$comment: SMGP-world team profiles (motto + ~5-paragraph history + quotes), keyed by team id. DISPLAY-ONLY (never a fold input, like the rival quotes / almanac / news corpora): shown on the promotion/demotion screen when the player joins a team. Fully fictional SEGA universe. Canon: Madonna = the crown (A. Senna), Bullets = the insurgent (G. Ceara), Zeroforce = the LEVEL D floor. Authored + adversarially canon/voice-verified by a 48-agent workflow.

team.madonna.name: Madonna
team.madonna.motto: YELLOW AND RED, FIRST AND FLAWLESS, THE CROWN NEVER SLIPS.
history: Before there was a ladder to climb, there was Madonna sitting at the top of it. The oldest royal house on the grid was gilded from its very first green flag, and it has never once been asked to prove it belongs, it simply arrives, and the paddock parts. Founded as the establishment team of the Super Monaco circuit, Madonna does not chase seasons; it collects them. The crown, they say, was not won here so much as inherited, and the house has spent every year since making certain no one forgets it.

Everything about Madonna is imperious, cold, and immaculate. Where other teams sweat and shout, the yellow-and-red garage moves in hushed, surgical calm, as though victory were a formality already signed and dated. This is the benchmark the entire field measures itself against: every lap time on the grid is quietly compared to the number one car, and every rival's dream is, at heart, a dream of dethroning this exact machine. Aristocratic to the bone, Madonna treats a podium as a homecoming and a defeat as a clerical error to be corrected by the next hairpin.

The machine itself is a golden weapon dressed in royal livery, deep imperial red bleeding into blazing gold, the number 1 worn not as a target but as a birthright. It is the best-prepared car in the paddock, its reliability legendary, breakdowns treated as scandals rather than misfortunes. Under the Monaco sun the yellow shell throws off light like a crown catching the flash of a thousand cameras, and drivers behind it learn to hate that glare in their mirrors. When it sings through the tunnel, the grandstands already know the result.

In the house's golden age the wheel belonged to A. Senna, the king, the untouchable star of this world, serene and inevitable, the man the whole grid chased and the man none of them could catch. Beside him rode A. Asselin, the perfect number two, a lieutenant polished for a single purpose: to close the door on anyone who dared imagine daylight past the crown. Together they formed a two-car wall of yellow and red, first and second so often that the timing screens read like a royal decree. Senna does not defend his lead; he simply owns the road, and the road knows it.

From its throne at Level A, Madonna looks down on a court of pretenders, the theatrical Firenze, the loud money of Millions, the clinical Bestowal, none of whom have ever truly loosened the crown. The only shadow it deigns to notice comes clawing up from far below: Bullets and the insurgent it raised, G. Ceara, the hungry outsider who races as though the number one seat already has his name on it. Madonna answers such ambition the way kings always have, with silence, distance, and another flawless win. The crown never slips; it only grows heavier for everyone else who tries to reach it.
quotes: Principal, to the assembled press: 'We do not enter races to compete. We enter them to confirm the order of things. The number one car leaves the box, and the season simply arranges itself accordingly. You are welcome to watch.'

The garage chief, wiping a spotless nose cone: 'Everyone else builds a race car. We build the standard the race car is judged against. When our machine coughs once in a decade, it makes the front page, that is how rarely the crown is allowed to bleed.'

A rival's dig, from the Bullets camp: 'They call it untouchable. I call it a seat that's been sitting empty of a real challenge for too long. That gold paint? It scratches like anyone else's, Ceara's just the one bringing the file.'

A. Asselin, adjusting his gloves in the number two cockpit he made famous: 'The king takes the win. I take everyone who thinks they can reach him. By the time you see the hairpin, the door is already closed, and it was always going to be.'

team.firenze.name: Firenze
team.firenze.motto: SECOND TO THE CROWN, AND TO NO ONE ELSE.
history: Before the World Championship had a name, the House of Firenze already had a trophy room. Born of old merchant-money older than the sport itself, this is the grand-prix aristocrat that carried motor racing out of the coaching age and into the roar of the modern big board. When the ladder was first drawn, no hand dared pencil Firenze anywhere but the very summit of LEVEL A, the house was there before the ladder existed. Old money does not audition for its seat; it endows the room the audition is held in.

Firenze does not race so much as PERFORM. Where the horsepower merchants bludgeon and the street-fighters brawl, the wine-dark cars of Firenze glide, every apex a brushstroke, every pit stop a piece of theatre for the faithful. The grandstand crowd that packs its stands doesn't cheer a car, they cheer a bloodline, and in the house's golden age the great I. Germi fed them opera with every lap: the tyre nursed like a jewel, the line threaded like a needle, the whole stand rising as one. This is the connoisseur's team, breeding over brute force, artistry over aggression, and a swagger only centuries of winning can buy.

And win it does. Germi's Firenze OWNED the tight twisting rounds, where pole was half the race and no man minted track position like the crowd's darling; it made the flat-out power circuits SHAKE, wing trimmed to nothing, the low-drag gamble run to the very edge with F. Elssler riding ice-cold shotgun. Yet for all its grandeur the house carries one eternal wound, it is second to the crown. Above it forever sits Madonna and the untouchable A. Senna, and the aristocrat's oldest agony is that no amount of breeding has ever quite reached the king. Firenze is the finest house on the grid that isn't the first, a champion doomed to hunt a crown it can see but never seize.

The machine is a jewel. Firenze runs the same top-spec chassis the crown itself trusts, but no one turns it out like this garage, wine-dark and antique gold, an ivory nose, the heraldic lily gleaming on the cowl like the family crest it truly is. Every panel is polished to a mirror, every bolt torqued like a watchmaker's work, the whole car a piece of Renaissance silverwork that happens to do three hundred. When the wine-and-gold machine fires in the pit lane, the faithful swear the sound alone is worth the price of the ticket.

Its rivalries are the rivalries of a grandee. Firenze looks UP at only one team, Madonna, the crown it chases every summer and curses every winter. It looks sideways, coolly, at the other aristocrats: Iris and its purple upstart pace, Azalea's flying pink precision, loud-mouthed Millions money and Bestowal's cold-steel poise, grandees all, held politely at arm's length. And it looks DOWN the ladder to where the insurgent Bullets first clawed up from LEVEL C behind the impossible G. Ceara, the one threat the old house pretends not to fear, and fears most of all. This is Firenze: second throne of the establishment, the aristocrat's aristocrat, the eternal, elegant, wine-dark shadow of the crown.
quotes: We do not chase races, signori, we RECEIVE them, as this house has received them since before your grandfathers could drive. Let the newcomers sweat and shout. Breeding never hurries, and Firenze never has to., the Firenze principal

You want the secret? We do not build a car, we RESTORE a masterpiece, every bolt a brushstroke, every stop a symphony. Give a driver like Germi a clean floor and that antique gold in the sun, and Firenze will make the whole grid weep., the Firenze garage
All that lovely wine-and-gold, all that breeding, and still they finish second. A crown you inherit is furniture, amigo. A crown you STEAL is a crown., G. Ceara, the Bullets insurgent
Firenze is the most beautiful thing on the grid. We do so enjoy watching it in our mirrors., the Madonna pit wall

team.millions.name: Millions
team.millions.motto: IF MONEY CAN BUY IT, THE FRONT ROW IS ALREADY OURS.
history: Millions was never built in a garage, it was bought, wholesale, the day a fortune too big to count decided racing looked like fun. Where rival teams begged and bartered for parts, the money men of Millions simply purchased the finest of everything: the sharpest engineers, the deepest wind-tunnel hours, the rawest young talent, all wheeled in on a tide of cash. From its very first roll onto the grid the team announced itself in gold leaf and gleaming chrome, a machine that cost more than half the paddock combined. There was no humble beginning here, only a chequebook, opened wide, and a promise to spend whatever it took to run at the sharp end.

The Millions creed is simple and unashamed: if a thing can win races, buy it, then buy the next one too, so no one else can have it. The team hoards advantages like a dragon hoards treasure, out-bidding the establishment for every edge before its rivals even know it exists. Its first great two-man line-up was pure brash top-tier swagger, loud and gilded and allergic to modesty, headlined by the roaring Brit N. Jones in the #5, who brought a mouth to match the money, and steadied by the patrician old-money G. Alberti in the #6, who has forgotten more podiums than most drivers will ever see. Together they wore the wealth like armour and dared the grid to say one word about it.

For all that gold, Millions is a genuine LEVEL A aristocrat: front-row regulars, race winners, a team the whole establishment fears on a fast Sunday. Yet there is one thing the chequebook has never managed to buy, and it gnaws under all the glitter, the crown itself. Race after race the Millions machine hunts down the field, only to find Madonna's yellow-and-red sitting one length ahead, A. Senna serenely uncatchable, wearing the one title money simply cannot purchase. They have bought the fastest of very nearly everything; they have never once bought the king's throne, and the entire paddock knows it.

The Millions machine, the gleaming g3-M3, is the most ostentatious sight on the big board, a rolling statement of net worth. Mirror-chrome flanks catch the Monaco sun like cut glass, wrapped in deep cash-green and dripping gold trim, every panel lacquered to a showroom shine and plastered with more sponsor names than the eye can read. Nothing about it is subtle, and that is entirely the point. When the #5 and #6 come howling out of the tunnel, the grandstand doesn't just see a race car, it sees money moving at two hundred miles an hour, and it cannot look away.

Above them sits Madonna and the crown money can't touch; beside them jostle the other blue-blood establishment names, Firenze, Bestowal, the flying purple Irises, each a rival for every prized signature and every drop of prestige. But the enemy that truly stings wears LEVEL C colours: the insurgent Bullets G. Ceara made famous, a giant-killer running front-rank pace on pocket change, which delights in nothing more than beating the richest car on the grid with a machine it could never afford. Every time that poor man's rocket embarrasses the gold, Millions simply writes a bigger cheque and swears next time will be different. In a ladder where the establishment guards its crown, Millions is the loudest, gaudiest, hungriest spender of them all, forever one purchase short of the only prize it truly wants.
quotes: Principal: You want our strategy? We find the fastest thing money can buy, then we buy the second-fastest too, so nobody else can have it. Around here the front row isn't earned, it's invoiced.

Chief mechanic: Everything on this car is the best that exists, and if it isn't, it will be by Friday, I fill in an order form and a nicer part just shows up. Gold-plated wheel nuts? Somebody upstairs said 'why not.' Only thing I can't requisition is Senna's helmet.
G. Ceara (the Bullets insurgent), rival dig: Nice chrome. Nice gold. Shame you can't buy a way past me at the hairpin. Money bought you the car, pal, it never once bought you the win.
N. Jones (then the #5): Millions bought the car, I brought the MOUTH, and this weekend I'm spending both! You don't out-drive the man with the fastest machine AND the biggest voice on the grid!

team.bestowal.name: Bestowal
history: Bestowal was not built, it was BESTOWED. The story on the grid says a grand old benefactor of the sport, richer than the record books can count, simply gifted a fully-funded seat at the very top and dared the world to be grateful. There was no garage in a back street, no scraped-together sponsors, no clawing up from the LEVEL D floor, Bestowal arrived at LEVEL A the way an heirloom arrives at a christening, already polished and already expected to shine. From the first green light it stood among the aristocrats, and it has never once considered standing anywhere else.

Understand the name and you understand the team: a bestowal is a GIFT, and a gift carries a debt. Everything here, the money, the machinery, the front-row seat, was handed over, so the only currency Bestowal trades in is EXPECTATION. Where other teams celebrate a podium, Bestowal merely notes that the podium was owed. The paddock calls them cold; the team calls it discipline. There is no fairy-tale here, no plucky underdog grin, only the patrician certainty of people raised to believe that winning is simply good manners.

The glory cabinet gleams with the weight of decades, championships collected like tribute, records set and quietly filed away where no rival can gloat over them. In the modern era all that legacy wore a single cold face: M. BLUME, car number 8, the metronome of the grid. He does not attack the hairpin; he CALCULATES it, brakes to the centimetre, and posts the same devastating lap over and over until the opposition simply runs out of race. 'No nerves, no error, no mercy,' the crews murmured, and lap after lap the timing screens kept agreeing.

The machine is German steel made visible: the gunmetal-and-silver missile that carried the great red 8 on its flanks, engineered rather than merely built. Its heart is raw HORSEPOWER, a brute of an engine that wakes up on the long forest blasts and the endless straights, where lesser cars lift and pray and Bestowal just plants the throttle. Down the slipstream wars it howls into the stadium bowl with the crowd on its feet, aerodynamically flawless and mechanically merciless. It is not a beautiful car. It is a CORRECT one, and correctness wins championships.

At the summit Bestowal answers to only one shadow: the yellow-and-red crown of Madonna, where A. Senna sits untouchable, the one benchmark all the establishment's money cannot simply purchase. Against Firenze's operatic fire Bestowal offers ice, against the loud new money of Millions it offers breeding, and it watches the insurgents clawing up from below, the Bullets of this world, with the wary contempt of an heir who has learned to distrust ambition. Its private terror is never spoken aloud: that the gift becomes a cage, that a team GIVEN everything has everything to lose. But at LEVEL A no one admits to fear. You simply win, because winning is what was bestowed.
quotes: Principal: 'We were not given this seat to warm it. We were given it to WIN from it, anything less is an insult to the gift, and Bestowal does not insult its benefactors.'
The garage: 'Senna has flair. Germi has fire. We have TOLERANCES. Blume's car left this garage correct to the thousandth of an inch, and the stopwatch did all the bragging we needed.'
M. Blume, then the #8: 'I do not race the grid, I model it. The outcome of my number 8 was calculated before the lights went out. You are simply here to confirm the numbers.'

A rival's dig (G. Ceara, the Bullets insurgent): 'Bestowal never EARNED a thing, they inherited a fast car and a bored face to sit in it. I'm going to take everything they were handed, one seat at a time.'

team.iris.name: Iris
team.iris.motto: CUT LIKE GLASS. SHINE LIKE A JEWEL. WIN LIKE IRIS.
history: Iris was born in a glassblower's quarter, founded by artisans who believed a race machine should be assembled the way a jeweler sets a stone, no seam allowed to show, no gram left unaccounted for. From its first season it carried itself not as a brawler but as a craftsman among crown teams, taking its place in LEVEL A on the strength of precision rather than swagger. Where the establishment measured itself in noise, Iris measured itself in tenths, and the tenths always came. The paddock learned quickly that the quiet team in the polished paddock bay was the one to fear on a clean, fast lap.

The character of Iris is cold clarity: cool heads, immaculate pit boxes, engineers in white gloves who treat a loose bolt as a personal insult. It does not chase drama the way the other aristocrats do; it chases the perfect corner, the flawless stop, the lap with nothing left in it. This makes Iris the connoisseur's crown team, the one the sharpest drivers dream of, the one that never wastes a machine or a moment. Rivals call it clinical; Iris calls it respect for the craft.

The machine is the whole point, a jewel-cut chassis in cool silver and deep iris-violet, every panel finished to a mirror so bright the grandstands see themselves in it. The livery catches the light like a faceted gem, a slash of violet down the flank the only flourish on an otherwise surgical body. It is the sleekest, most precise car on the grid, tuned to knife through the fast sweepers where lesser machines wash wide. When Iris comes past on a qualifying lap, it does not roar so much as sing, a clean, high, expensive note.

Iris lives among the LEVEL A crown teams, forever measuring itself against Madonna's untouchable yellow-and-red at the very top. It concedes that the crown belongs to A. Senna and Madonna for now, but it concedes nothing else, it will out-qualify, out-brake, and out-finish every other aristocrat to be the closest jewel to the throne. Its rivalry with the establishment is one of style: the brute confidence of the old guard against the cut-glass perfection of Iris. To beat Madonna cleanly, on pace, with no error, that is the whole ambition of the team.

Season after season Iris banks podiums the way its founders banked polished stones: quietly, steadily, building a treasury of results that speaks louder than any boast. It has known glory in the fast years and frustration in the ones where raw power beat finesse, but it has never known shame, and it has never slipped from LEVEL A. The insurgents down the ladder study Iris to learn how a top team should carry itself; the crown studies Iris because it is always, always right behind. In this world Iris is the standard of precision, the jewel the whole grid tries to match, and cannot quite.
quotes: Principal: 'We do not build a fast car. We build a perfect one, and perfect happens to be fast. Bring me a lap with a single wasted centimeter and I will show you the door.'
Garage: 'White gloves on, gentlemen. If you can see a fingerprint on that violet, it is not going on the grid. We set this machine like a stone, not a sandwich.'
Rival's dig (Madonna): 'Iris polishes. Senna wins. Let them keep buffing that mirror behind us, it makes a lovely view of our gearbox.'
Principal: 'Madonna wears the crown. Fine. We will be the jewel one step below it, cut so clean the crown never dares relax.'

team.azalea.name: Azalea
team.azalea.motto: PETALS OF STEEL, SHE BLOOMS WHEN THE LIGHTS BURN BRIGHTEST.
history: Azalea did not claw its way onto the grid, it was CULTIVATED onto it. Where lesser teams are bought or bolted together, Azalea's machine was raised like a rare bloom in a glasshouse, hand-shaped panel by panel until it was ready to open. It arrived at LEVEL A twinned with its sister marque, Iris, two jewels grown from the same root and set among the aristocrats on the very first day. Nobody called them upstarts. From the moment the covers came off, Azalea belonged with the crown-chasers, poised, perfumed, and quietly lethal.

The team's whole character is one held breath. Azalea does not waste itself on the early rounds; it lets the loudmouths bang wheels while it waits, cool and patient, for the stage worth flowering on. Then the marquee weekend comes, the night race, the hairpin under the floodlights, the title-deciding finale, and the bloom OPENS. Rivals who dismissed it as decorative in round three find it towering over them in round sixteen, and by then it is far, far too late.

Look at the machine and you would swear it belongs in a vase, not a pit lane. The livery is blossom and frost, deep magenta petals fanning into cold white, chrome edges catching the sun like dew, every line drawn for elegance. But run a hand along that bodywork and you feel the truth: petals of steel over an engine that snarls. It is the most beautiful car on the grid and the one that ends the most seasons, and Azalea likes that contradiction very much.

In the cockpit of the house's first great era sat Mika Larssen, the ice at the heart of the flower, calm to the point of cruelty behind the number 34. Larssen does not blink, does not boast, does not celebrate, the win is simply collected and the cold returns. That temperament defined Azalea's place in the ladder: it stood beside Madonna's crown as the OTHER jewel of LEVEL A, the one machine that made even the King's garage check its mirrors on the grand nights. It does not shout at Madonna and it does not pretend to wear the crown, it simply waits for the biggest moment and blooms over everyone foolish enough to have written it off.

There is a gentle war within the family, too, Iris headlines with a showman's grin while Azalea works in silence, the loud sister and the glacial one, forever measured against each other. But on the grand nights the whole paddock turns to the frost-and-magenta car, because everyone knows where the danger truly grows. Azalea's trophy cabinet is not the fullest on the grid; it is the one stuffed with the BIGGEST rounds, the ones that decide everything. When it counts, and only when it counts, the coldest flower on the grid comes into full and terrible bloom.
quotes: Principal: 'We do not chase the small rounds, we cultivate. Let the others bang wheels in the spring. When the lights burn their brightest, Azalea opens, and the grid finally understands what patience grows.'
The garage, wiping a smudge from the magenta flank: 'Every petal on this car is measured to the gram. It looks like it should be in a vase. It is built to end your season.'
Mika Larssen, then the #34, helmet still on: 'I do not celebrate. I bloom, I win, I return to the cold.'
A rival from the crown's camp, sneering down the pit lane: 'A flower? On MY grid? How quaint. Somebody fetch a vase, the hairpin's about to press it flat.'

team.blanche.name: Blanche
team.blanche.motto: SPOTLESS BODYWORK. SPOTLESS RECORD. ONE PASS FROM THE CROWN.
history: BLANCHE is born from a single obsession -- that a race car should cross the finish line exactly as spotless as it left the garage. Its founder, a perfectionist who could not abide a fingerprint on chrome, painted the first machine the purest white on the grid and dared the dust of Monaco to touch it. From that very first season the team refused sponsors' clutter, refused theatrics, refused every shortcut the establishment took for granted. What Blanche built instead was a reputation: the most disciplined outfit below the crown, and the most maddeningly close to reaching it.

There is no shouting in the Blanche garage -- only the click of torque wrenches and the hush of people who measure everything twice. The mechanics are called 'the whitecoats,' and they earn the name: gloves on, floor mopped between sessions, every bolt logged and every lap dissected to the thousandth. The team treats a race weekend like surgery, cold and exact, and it treats a single mistake like a stain that must never be allowed to appear twice. Rivals mock the fuss -- then they watch a white car run sixteen flawless rounds and quietly stop laughing.

For all that precision, Blanche lives on the wrong side of one door. It stands at the very top of LEVEL B, close enough to the aristocrats of Madonna and Firenze to smell the champagne, never quite close enough to pour it. Season after season the white cars collect podiums, poles and heartbreak -- beaten to the crown not by chaos but by a tenth, by a budget, by the sheer gravity of the establishment above. The team does not sulk; it files the defeat, cleans the car and returns a fraction sharper, because Blanche believes the door to LEVEL A opens only for whoever is spotless when it finally swings.

The machine itself is a statement -- bodywork so white it glares under the Monaco sun, race numbers in crisp black, not a smear of gaudy sponsorship anywhere on the panels. On track it looks less like a race car than a surgeon's blade: clean lines, zero drama, lethally quick through the fast stuff. The engineers tune for consistency over heroics, so the car rarely wins the lottery of a wet gamble but almost never throws a result away. When it flashes past the pit wall unmarked after a full Grand Prix distance, that single image is the whole brand.

Blanche's fiercest feud is a study in opposites -- the pristine white against the bludgeon of TYRANT, polish versus brawl, both clawing to be the first LEVEL B team promoted into the crown's company. Above them Madonna and the Level A elite treat Blanche as the well-mannered nuisance forever wiping its feet at their threshold. Below, the ambitious kids climbing out of LEVEL C learn fast that the white car is the first real gatekeeper -- beat it twice and you have genuinely arrived. Even the grinning number ten of those years swore the crown was coming any race now; the pit wall just smiled, tightened one more bolt, and kept the machine spotless for the day it finally would.
quotes: THE PRINCIPAL: 'WE DO NOT GAMBLE, WE DO NOT SHOUT, WE DO NOT STAIN. WE ARRIVE CLEAN AND WE FINISH SECOND TO NO ONE BUT THE CROWN -- AND ONLY FOR NOW.'
THE GARAGE: 'GLOVES ON. FLOOR MOPPED. EVERY BOLT LOGGED. WHEN THAT CAR COMES HOME WITHOUT A MARK ON IT, THAT'S THE WHITECOATS SIGNING THEIR WORK.'
A MADONNA RIVAL'S DIG: 'BLANCHE? THE PRETTIEST RUNNER-UP ON THE GRID. KEEP POLISHING, DARLING -- THE CROWN HAS NEVER ONCE COME IN WHITE.'
A TYRANT RIVAL'S DIG: 'ALL THAT SPOTLESS PAINT AND NOT ONE SCRATCH -- BECAUSE BLANCHE NEVER DARES A MOVE HARD ENOUGH TO EARN ONE. I'LL PUT THE FIRST DENT IN IT MYSELF, AT THE HAIRPIN.'

team.tyrant.name: Tyrant
team.tyrant.motto: OBEY THE IRON, OR FEED IT TO THE WALL.
history: Tyrant did not so much form as SEIZE its place on the grid. When a floundering LEVEL D backmarker collapsed one winter, a cold-eyed crew of ex-enforcers bought the wreckage for scrap money, tore the old name off the nose, and bolted on their own in raw iron letters, TYRANT. From day one the creed was carved in metal: the road owes you nothing, so you take it by force. They clawed out of the basement in a single brutal campaign, and the midfield has feared the gunmetal cars ever since.

There is no charm in the Tyrant garage, and none is offered. The pit wall runs like a boot camp, no excuses, no passengers, no mercy for a tenth left on the table, and a driver who lifts is a driver who is already being measured for replacement. On track the cars drive like a threat: they arrive on your gearbox, lean on your door, and dare you to blink first. Where softer teams race for points, Tyrant races to intimidate, and the whole of LEVEL C has learned to check its mirrors when the gunmetal pair come hunting.

For all their menace, Tyrant lives on the wrong side of a locked door. They are LEVEL B, strong enough to bloody any nose on the tour, hungry enough to hammer at the establishment every single weekend, yet the crown of LEVEL A keeps the bolt thrown against them. Their glory came in raids, not reigns: M. Hamano owning the bridge on his home day, reading the crossover like his own pulse; G. Gould stealing the island in the very shadow of the wall of kings. But when the season's numbers are read, the aristocrats of Madonna and Firenze are still up the road, and nothing on this earth enrages the Tyrant pit wall more.

The machine is a Gen-3 classic bruiser dressed to threaten, gunmetal grey slabbed over oxblood red, a black iron fist stamped on each flank, rivet lines picked out along the bodywork like a set of knuckles. It is set up hard and late, built around the one thing Tyrant trusts above all else: the brakes. On the crossover bridges and the wall-lined chicanes the car becomes a weapon, diving a heartbeat deeper than sense allows and daring the Armco to argue the point. It is not the prettiest thing in the paddock, it is simply the one you least want to see filling your mirror.

Tyrant fights a two-front war, and relishes both. Below them they lord it over LEVEL C, leaning on Dardan and Linden and the strivers for sport, and glaring hardest at the rising Bullets, that hungry Brazilian upstart the Tyrant wall would love nothing more than to stamp back down before he ever climbs past them. Beside them, Blanche, Losel and May trade blows for the honor of knocking first at the top. And above, always above, hangs the establishment's shut door, and every gunmetal weekend is one more fist thrown against it, harder than the last.
quotes: "This paddock loves the phrase 'racing incident.' We don't use it. There are drivers who move, and there are drivers who get moved, decide which one you are before you line up beside my cars.", V. Kessler, Tyrant principal

"Boss don't want the car fast. Boss wants it FRIGHTENING. We build the brakes to hold one lap longer than the other fella's nerve, everything after that takes care of itself.", a Tyrant chief mechanic, spanner in hand

"Tyrant? Big iron fist for the boys below, soft little bow at the door above. They'll flatten the whole of C just to feel tall, then park nice and quiet behind Madonna like they always do.", a LEVEL C rival, unimpressed
"They point me at the wall of kings and call it a corner. On my island, they happen to be right.", G. Gould, then the Tyrant #12

team.losel.name: Losel
team.losel.motto: COLD HANDS. HOT LAPS. WE DON'T GUESS, WE CALCULATE.
history: Losel was born in a wind tunnel, not a boardroom. Its founders were run-out engineers and slide-rule fanatics who believed a race is won on the drawing board weeks before the lights go out, and they built a team that treats every corner as an equation with exactly one correct answer. From day one the Losel garage ran cooler and quieter than any on the grid, no shouting, no drama, just the hum of stopwatches and the scratch of pencils. That icy discipline earned them a place in LEVEL B faster than anyone expected, and it never left.

The character of Losel is precision worn like armor. Where LEVEL D strugglers pray and LEVEL A aristocrats preen, Losel simply measures, tire temperatures, fuel loads, the exact angle of a driver's helmet in a crosswind. They are famous for never spending a single unit of effort they can't justify on a data sheet, which makes them maddening to race against and impossible to rattle. Rivals call them heartless; Losel calls it being right.

Their glory is the glory of the almost, the team that finishes the LEVEL B season a whisker from promotion, year after year, close enough to smell the crown but never quite gripping it. They have out-engineered half the LEVEL A establishment on pure pace and still watched the aristocrats keep the trophies, because raw calculation can carry you to the door of the elite but heart and history hold it shut. That gap is the great open wound of the Losel story, and the whole garage grinds at it every winter, convinced the next tenth of a second is the one that finally breaks the door down.

The Losel machine is a study in restraint: gunmetal grey wrapped in cold electric-blue pinstripes, every panel flush, every rivet accounted for, not one gram of paint spent on flash. It looks less like a race car and more like a precision instrument that happens to do 300, a scalpel among the grid's sledgehammers. On track it is eerily consistent, lap after identical lap, the blue needle threading the same perfect line until the tires simply run out of chemistry. Beautiful, in the way a proof is beautiful.

In the ladder Losel sits among the strong and the hungry, forever eyeing the LEVEL A crown that Madonna and A. Senna guard from the summit, and forever unsettled by the insurgent Bullets, whose driver G. Ceara won on pure fire the way Losel wins on pure math. That contrast defines them: the cold calculators versus the burning outsider, two ways of chasing the same untouchable Senna. Losel respects Madonna's dominance and quietly resents that respect isn't a podium. Their whole existence is a bet that, given enough data, even the crown becomes a solvable problem.
quotes: Principal: 'Passion loses tenths. We do not deal in passion, we deal in the answer, and the answer is always in the numbers. Hand me the data sheet and I will hand you the podium.'
Garage crew: 'Everyone else tunes by feel. We tune by proof. When that blue car leaves the box, it has already run the lap a thousand times in here, the track's just the formality.'

Rival's dig (Bullets): 'Losel's got the fastest calculator on the grid and the coldest heart. Ceara drives on fire, mate, you can't put a stopwatch on wanting it. That's why they measure the crown and we go take it.'
Principal: 'Madonna guards the summit with history. Fine. History is just data we haven't overtaken yet.'

team.may.name: May
team.may.motto: SPRING NEVER STOPS CLIMBING, AND NEITHER DO WE!
history: May opens its garage doors the morning the frost finally breaks, and the whole paddock swears you can smell blossom on the pit lane. Founded by a crew of grinning young hopefuls who pooled their savings for one green chassis and a dream, May is the team that arrived like a thaw, sudden, warm, and impossible to ignore. They painted the machine the fresh bright green of first leaves, ran a white blossom down the flanks, and rolled out to the LEVEL B fields with sunshine in their eyes. Nobody at the top took them seriously. Everybody does now.

The character of May is pure springtime optimism, and it drives their rivals up the wall. This is the youngest, brightest crew on the whole grid, mechanics who whistle while they wrench and a driver who waves at the crowd on the warm-up lap. Where other teams brood and scheme, May simply believes: believes the next corner is theirs, believes the sun is on their side, believes a team can bloom straight out of the midfield and into the crown. It is a dangerous kind of hope, because it never, ever wilts.

For all that brightness, May knows the ache of LEVEL B, the ache of finishing one place short of everything that matters. They have crashed the podium a handful of glorious times, sprayed the bubbly, and gone home to LEVEL B all the same, noses pressed to the glass of the establishment above. Season after season they climb: a place here, a scalp there, always green, always rising, never quite through the gate. But nobody in the paddock doubts where this is heading, May is not a team that has peaked, May is a team that is still growing, and growth like this ends in only one place.

The May machine is the freshest sight in the field: that vivid leaf-green body, the white blossom sweeping back from the nose, wheels that seem to skip rather than roll. It runs sweetest when the weather turns warm, give May a sun-baked afternoon and the green car comes alive, hunting down heavier, older machines on the long straights while its rivals fade in the heat. The livery is deliberately, cheerfully loud, a moving billboard for the idea that new blood belongs up front. Rivals call it garish. Fans call it the prettiest thing on the grid.

May's real war is with the flowers already crowned above them, Azalea and Iris, the pampered LEVEL A blooms who were handed the top garden long before May sprouted. To May, that is the whole insult: springtime belongs to the newcomer, not to hothouse aristocrats resting on old petals. Among the LEVEL B pack they trade blows with hardy Blanche and glowering Tyrant, but their eyes never leave the summit, where Madonna's yellow-and-red crown glints far, far up the ladder. May does not fear that distance. May just plants another season and grows.
quotes: Principal: 'They keep telling us to wait our turn. Son, spring doesn't wait, it arrives, and everything green comes up with it. LEVEL A had better learn our name before we paint it on the trophy.'

The garage: 'We tune this thing at dawn with the doors open so the car wakes up when we do. She loves the warm afternoons, you point her at the sun and that little green machine just BLOOMS down the straight!'
Azalea, LEVEL A, sniffing: 'A blossom team, how sweet. Blossoms are lovely for a week and then they blow away. We've been in this garden for years, darling, go grow up somewhere in LEVEL B.'

Rival at Tyrant, grumbling: 'That smiling green lot make you sick, waving at the crowd, whistling in the pits. Then the flag drops and the cheerful little thing is all over your gearbox. Somebody prune them before they reach the top.'

team.joke.name: Joke
team.joke.motto: THEY CAME TO LAUGH, THEY LEFT IN OUR MIRRORS.
history: They say Joke rolled into the paddock as a dare, a garish carnival outfit half the grid took for a publicity stunt, and a slow one at that. The grandees chuckled behind their pit boards on day one; by the finale of that first campaign the #16 had gatecrashed the points and wiped every smirk clean off the big board. What began as the circus's punchline muscled its way up the ladder into LEVEL B on sheer, stinging pace, and it has been hammering on the establishment's front door ever since. Nobody on this tour laughs at Joke twice.

The team's whole art is misdirection. In the Preliminary Race they will sandbag and clown and dawdle a full second off the pace, lulling the field into scratching the harlequin off the reckoning, then Sunday's flag drops and the punchline arrives at the hairpin with the throttle buried. No pit wall on the calendar can tell you whether the carnival crew is serious until it is already past you, and that not-knowing is worth as much to them as raw horsepower. They are theatrical, they are unpredictable, and underneath the grease-paint they are genuinely, dangerously quick.

The machine itself is dressed to unsettle, a harlequin motley of mismatched diamonds, a grinning jester mask leering off the nose cone, colours that clash on purpose so a rival's eye can never quite settle on it in the mirrors. It looks like a gag rolled straight out of a fairground, right up until it strips your D.P. and vanishes up the road. At the wheel in the team's first great era sat L. Dufay, the paddock's chief prankster, the man who waved at you as he went by and swore blind he greased the hairpin himself. The livery is the joke; the lap time is the twist in the tale.

For all the clowning, Joke earns its LEVEL B keep the hard way, on the podium, and in other men's nightmares about the championship arithmetic. They are the great spoilers of the circus: the outfit that lifts a rostrum step off a grandee mid-title-fight and turns a coronation into a farce nobody up top finds funny. Twice or thrice a season the harlequin rattles the crockery in the LEVEL A drawing rooms, close enough to make the crown flinch, yet the throne itself still belongs to A. Senna's Madonna, and Joke has never once been let all the way inside. That is the itch this team cannot scratch: they can ruin a king's afternoon, but they cannot yet take his chair.

The king does not laugh, which is precisely why Joke lives to make Madonna sweat. Against the rest of the LEVEL B pack, the stern Tyrant, the patient Losel, the polished Blanche, the workmanlike May, it is open warfare for the right to be the next name promoted, and the tricksters fight it with banana-skin racecraft the others find maddening. They feel a certain kinship with the Bullets G. Ceara made famous, that other outsider clawing at the grandees, though where the insurgent hunts the crown in deadly earnest, Joke would rather pinch it while everyone is still chuckling. To the whole grid, watching the harlequin loom up in the mirrors is the worst news on the big board, because nobody, not even Joke, knows what the punchline will be. Strong, rising, impossible to plan around: that is their rung on the ladder.
quotes: "You paid for a comedy, ladies and gentlemen, and a comedy you'll get, the twist is the joke lands on whoever qualified ahead of the harlequin. Enjoy the show.", the Ringmaster, working the tannoy like a fairground barker.
"We tune the engine, we polish the mask, and yes, we may have oiled the hairpin. Prove it.", the Joke garage, grins under the grease.
"A fairground float with a steering wheel, send the clowns back to the tent.", a LEVEL A grandee, moments before the harlequin relieved him of a podium step.
"Check your mirrors, friend, that grinning fella waving? That's your afternoon, leaving.", L. Dufay, then the #16.

team.bullets.name: Bullets
team.bullets.motto: LOADED AT LEVEL C. AIMED STRAIGHT AT THE CROWN.
history: Bullets does not descend from money, it was hammered together in a backstreet lock-up by men who could not afford a seat at the grandees' table, so they built a weapon instead of a showpiece. The name was a promise: light, loaded, and pointed at something bigger than itself. When they went hunting for a driver mad enough to fire it, they found a young Brazilian named Gilberto Ceara, and the garage has not been quiet a single day since. From that first season the little gunmetal machine has carried a chip the size of the crown itself.

Here is the thing the establishment cannot forgive: Bullets is stamped LEVEL C, and it drives like LEVEL A. Ceara is A. Senna's one true equal, the stopwatch says so, race after race, the two of them breaking clear of the whole field and trading the lead like a dare. The entire ladder measures the gap between the insurgent and the king, and keeps coming back with the same impossible answer: there isn't one. A midfield garage has no business running up there with the grandees. Nobody at Bullets has ever cared for a second.

For all its fire, Bullets has never lifted the crown, the king's team does not let go, and the World Championship has slipped the insurgent's grasp more than once with the title in plain sight. But ask any grandstand and they will name the days that matter: the sweltering home round where Ceara dragged the Bullets past cars worth a king's ransom while the crowd screamed itself hoarse, and the wet afternoons where a LEVEL C machine simply vanished up the road into the spray. This is a team that wins the day outright or breaks trying, glory and heartbreak living in the same overworked gearbox. The crown never slips. Bullets is the reason it sweats.

The Bullets #17 looks like exactly what it is called: a round chambered and ready, gunmetal flanks, a brass blade down the nose, a flash of tropical green for the man they found to fire it. The garage builds it light to the edge of recklessness, snapping it off the line quicker than anything on the grid and knifing it through standing water as if the rain were laid on for its benefit alone. The price is written in smoke, because a machine stripped that fine rides its gearbox on a highwire, and a Bullets weekend is a coin spun between a famous win and a plume on the horizon. They would not build it any other way.

Everything about Bullets bends toward one man in a yellow-and-red Madonna. The Senna-Ceara war is personal, and it is the finest show the circus owns, nose to nose through the finale tunnel, side by side into the last chicane, a whole season boiling down to who lifts first. The grandees at LEVEL A look down the ladder and see the single garage that genuinely frightens them, clawing upward one round at a time with the crown fixed in its crosshairs. Senna takes the flag, he always seems to. But the insurgent is patient, and a loaded gun only has to be right once.
quotes: Principal: "We read LEVEL C on the paperwork and LEVEL A on the stopwatch, and the crown knows the difference. Give me a Ceara, a full tank, and a black sky, and I'll show you precisely where the establishment keeps its nerves."

Garage chief: "She's not built to finish pretty, friend, she's built to finish FIRST. We chamber her light, strip her to the bone, and pray the box holds to the flag. And when the heavens crack open? You'd best say your prayers to somebody else, because that weather belongs to us."

A Madonna man's dig: "Cute. The little gun barks on its home straight, barks in the rain, and calls it a coronation. Ceara has led my king onto the final lap a hundred times and watched the #1 pull away a hundred times. The crown does not slip for LEVEL C."

Ceara: "They handed me a midfield car and a name like a bullet. Perfect, I only need the one shot, and the calendar hands me sixteen. Senna takes the flag, for now. The crown never slips... but I am the reason it never sleeps."

team.dardan.name: Dardan
team.dardan.motto: RAISE THE SPEAR, DARDAN NEVER KNEELS.
history: They say the Dardan works were forged, not founded, hammered together in a hillside garage by a clan that measured pride in scars and settled arguments with wrenches. The name is old and warlike, drawn from the Dardanian mountain folk who never met a border they wouldn't charge, and the team wears that heritage like war-paint. From its very first race Dardan announced itself not as a supplicant begging entry to the great houses but as a raiding party sent to bloody their noses. In the SMGP paddock it is the outfit that arrives spoiling for a fight, proud, prickly, and gloriously certain it belongs a level higher than the timing screens will admit.

The machine is the g3m4, the same brawling chassis half of Level C fields, but at Dardan it is dressed for battle: oxblood red over hammered bronze, a spearhead flash running nose to tail like a blade drawn down the bodywork. The crew treats the car as a weapon rather than an ornament; they would sooner see it come home dented and third than spotless and eighth. Its livery carries no soft sponsor gloss, only the clan spear and one defiant number, and it looks, frankly, like it wants to hurt something. On a tight, walled street circuit, Dardan's favourite kind of knife-fight, that bronze nose filling your mirrors is a promise, not a threat.

Dardan has never worn the crown, and it knows it, the great houses of Level A were built on marble, while Dardan was built on grudges. This is a midfield warlord, forever besieging the establishment from below, and Level C is precisely where its reputation is forged, race by bloody race. Yet it carries one strange and savage honour on the grid: when a fallen champion is stripped of Madonna and cast out, it is Dardan's garage that catches them. The team takes the crownless, presses a spear back into their hands, and dares them to climb, because being dumped at Dardan is not the end of a career, it is the opening shot of a war.

Dardan's blood feud is an intimate one: it shares Level C, and the very same g3m4 machine, with Bullets, G. Ceara's rising insurgents, and the two teams cannot pass on track without throwing sparks. Where Ceara's mob claws upward as hungry outsiders, Dardan claws with a chip on both shoulders, each side convinced the other is squatting on a seat that should be theirs. Above them looms Level B, Blanche, Tyrant, Losel, May, Joke, the door Dardan has spent its whole existence trying to kick down. And when some ambitious young charger climbs up out of Minarae to make a name, Dardan is the wall they must break through first; many a reputation has been measured against that bronze spear and found wanting.

In the garage's modern era the spear was carried by Eddie Bellini in the #18 and Keke Alfven in the #19, a snarling veteran and a cold-eyed wheelman who between them treated every corner as contested ground. The team's ambition is naked and unapologetic: not merely to survive Level C but to storm out of it, drag a Level B seat down into the dirt, and never once look back. Every season Dardan lines up as the proudest scrappers on the grid, undersized in budget, oversized in nerve, spoiling to embarrass machinery that costs ten times as much. Raise the spear, drop the clutch: the midfield is Dardan's battlefield, and it races like a team with a crown to avenge.
quotes: Principal Vukan Dardanos: "Madonna wears a crown; we wear scars. Ask the grid which of the two survives the first corner."
The Forge, chief mechanic: "We don't polish this car, we sharpen it. She rolls out of my garage looking for somebody to hit."
A Firenze aristocrat, sneering: "Dardan? A charming little war-band. It's where we send fallen kings to be forgotten, big proud spear, permanent Level C."
Eddie Bellini, then the #18: "They dumped me at Dardan like it's a grave. Good. I'll climb out with the spear in my teeth and take back every seat they cost me."

team.linden.name: Linden
team.linden.motto: ROOTS TO THE BEDROCK, THE LINDEN NEVER FALLS.
history: Linden is the oldest workshop still turning a wheel on the Super Monaco GP grid, raised in the shade of a single great linden tree that has outlived every principal who ever parked beneath it. The team was founded generations ago by a family of wheelwrights and clockmakers who swore a racing car should be built the way you plant a tree, slowly, deeply, to stand for a hundred seasons. They put no man's name on the garage door, only the tree's, because a name can retire but a root keeps growing. Rivals laughed at a stable that measured progress in decades; Linden simply kept turning up, calendar after calendar, long after the laughers had rusted away.

Linden is LEVEL C to its bark, and proud of it, never reaching for the aristocrats' crown, never sinking toward the floor's despair, just the steady green machine that is always, always there. Where Madonna chases immortality and the bottom teams claw at survival, Linden endures, banking points the way a farmer banks a harvest. Its engineers do not gamble a season on a wild setup; they season a car like timber, testing until it refuses to warp under pressure. The paddock jokes you can set your watch by a Linden result, unhurried, dependable, and stubbornly unbreakable.

Their trophy shelf holds no world championship, and they will tell you, unblinking, that they have never wanted one they'd have to bet the whole garage to steal. What Linden collects instead is time: more starts, more finishes, more seasons on this grid than any team beneath LEVEL A. Young chargers pass through the workshop on their climb up the ladder, and every one of them leaves smoother, steadier, and quietly grateful for whatever the old wood taught them. In a world drunk on the crown, Linden's whole reputation rests on the one thing money can never buy, roots.

The car is a deep forest-green machine, its flanks the colour of high-summer leaves darkening to bark at the nose, carrying the steady number 22 through every hairpin without a word of complaint. It is not the quickest thing in the field, but it is the last car you will ever see smoking against the Armco, reliability seasoned into every bolt. In the cockpit of the modern era sat Marcel Moreau, the paddock's resident philosopher, a driver who reads a circuit the way an old forester reads weather in the sky. He does not attack the hairpin; he waits for it to open, and speaks of Monaco as a river that will always bend for the patient man.

Linden's sharpest quarrel is with its own LEVEL C neighbour, Bullets, the hungry young insurgent that came up out of nowhere and now storms toward the crown, treating the old tree as so much deadwood to be burned past. Ceara's sapling has the raw speed; Linden has the seasons, and the two trade the midfield the way fire trades ground with old, wet timber. Far above, the crown glitters on Madonna's machine, and Linden watches it without a flicker of envy, certain the flashy always fall while the rooted remain. And whenever a rising star at Minarae or a soul stranded down at the floor looks up, they meet the same patient green that has weathered every storm on the calendar, and learn that some teams you beat, and some you simply have to outlive.
quotes: Principal: We do not chase the crown, and we do not fear the floor. A linden that lunges too fast for the sun snaps in the first real storm, so we grow, and we endure. Come back in ten seasons and count who is still standing.
Garage: Flashy? Not us, friend. But you will never once see number 22 walking home. We season every bolt like firewood, by the time it goes on the car, it's forgotten how to break.
Marcel Moreau (the old #22): The hairpin is a question the whole grid shouts at. I am the only one patient enough to wait for it to answer.
Rival's dig (Bullets): Linden? That's not a race team, that's a piece of furniture with a number on it. I'll have the crown fitted before that old stump finishes its warm-up lap.

team.minarae.name: Minarae
team.minarae.motto: EVERY CHAMPION STARTED IN A MINARAE.
history: Every climb needs a first rung you are PROUD to stand on, and that is the whole idea behind Minarae. Founded as the World Championship's front door, this little LEVEL C outfit exists to do one thing: hand a raw, unproven kid a real racing car and point him straight up the field. The team truck rolls into every paddock with the banner unfurled and the same three words stitched across the crew's overalls, 'LET'S TRY HARD TO WIN.' No dynasty, no crown gathering dust in a trophy case; just a garage that swears the next great name is always the one strapping into car #20.

Minarae is where the computer sends you when nobody in the paddock knows your name yet, and the grandees have long since learned not to laugh. This is the proving ground of the whole grid, the place reputations are MADE rather than inherited. A driver who shines here never stays long; he is poached up the ladder by the hungry LEVEL B teams, or he names a rival, beats him twice, and simply takes a better seat by force. The team wears that turnover as a badge of honour, a garage measured not by titles it keeps, but by talent it launches.

The machine is no titan: a modest formula car carrying more courage than horsepower, the kind you must WRESTLE rather than merely steer. Its livery is famous for its plainness, a big bold number on the nose and honest workshop colours, unmistakable amid the sponsor-plastered aristocrats precisely because it flaunts so little. What it lacks in straight-line thunder it repays in the twisty stuff, where a brave right foot outscores a fat budget every time. Reliable enough to see the flag, nimble enough to embarrass its betters through the barriers, the little Minarae is built to punch upward.

Its legend is written by drivers who flatly refuse to accept the midfield. B. Miller, the street-fighting American in car #20, turned the concrete canyon of the U.S.A. round into MINARAE COUNTRY, brake-late, elbows-out, kissing walls the grandees flinched from, until even the untouchable A. Senna found no clean air out front. Beside him, the jittery hopeful J. Nono of the #21 swore up and down that he eats hairpins for breakfast, and just often enough he actually did. Every rivalry the garage picks points the same way, UP: toward the LEVEL B door, and far above it the yellow-and-red Madonna crown that every Minarae kid stares at from the back of the pack.

Make no mistake, Minarae is not a home, it is an ON-RAMP. Nobody dreams of growing old in LEVEL C; the entire point is to earn your way OUT of it, one named rival and one hard-won seat at a time. The team knows this and loves it anyway, because a garage that keeps launching careers never truly grows old. So climb into that plain little car, remember the banner and its three painted words, and understand the deal: the whole championship you came to chase sits above you, and the only road out of Minarae runs one direction, UP.
quotes: 'We don't sell you a trophy, we sell you a STARTING LINE. Drive well enough and you won't be ours for long, and that suits us fine. That has ALWAYS been the plan.', the Minarae team principal
'She's plain, she's honest, and she'll bring you home if you don't hurl her at a wall. Now get in and buckle up. LET'S TRY HARD TO WIN.', the Minarae head mechanic, slapping the nose of car #20
'Minarae? Ha! That is not a team, my friend, that is a WAITING ROOM with wheels.', a LEVEL A grandee, looking all the way down the ladder
'This little car and a canyon full of walls? That is not a disadvantage. That is HOME FIELD. Leave me a gap and I'll take it.', B. Miller, then the #20

team.lares.name: Lares
team.lares.motto: EVERY DRIVER COMES HOME TO LARES!
history: Lares was founded not in a boardroom but in a family workshop, by old hands who believed a race team should feel like a household, a hearth where a driver is fed, sheltered, and never left out in the cold. From its first season it claimed LEVEL C not as a stepping stone but as a home worth defending, the warm middle of the ladder where a raw career finds its feet. While the crown teams chase glory and the floor teams claw for survival, Lares simply keeps the lamp lit and the garage door open. Theirs is a homely, unglamorous loyalty that the whole paddock quietly respects, even the ones who snicker at it.

The Lares machine is a stout, honest thing, deep hearth-red bodywork with a warm cream stripe running its length like a mantelpiece, and a small brass household crest set above the airbox. It is never the quickest car through the speed traps, but it is the one that always comes back running, lap after lap, race after race. Lares mechanics are famous for reliability the way other teams are famous for pole positions; a Lares car crossing the line is practically a law of nature. The livery draws no gasps on the grid walk, but it draws nods, that is the machine that will still be circulating at the flag.

Lares has never troubled A. Senna's Madonna crown and has never once pretended it might. Its glory is measured in a different currency: seasons survived, rookies taken in and made whole, points scavenged on the days the fancy teams broke down and limped to the pits. The team's proudest stretch was a run of finishes so dependable that rivals grumbled a Lares car would still be circulating long after the checkered flag, the crew, and the catering had all gone home. Around the LEVEL C campfires they call it the guardian's record, not the loudest legend on the ladder, but one of the steadiest.

Its rivalries are neighborly rather than venomous, the sharpest runs against Bullets, the hungry insurgent house of G. Ceara that shares the LEVEL C midfield. Where Bullets is all noise and knives-out ambition, sprinting to break into LEVEL B and hunt the crown, Lares is the patient neighbor who was here first and intends to still be here after. The two houses trade paint at every mid-grid scrap, Bullets snarling past on raw pace, Lares tucking back into the points once the dust settles. It is the friendliest feud on the ladder and, in its own stubborn way, the most eternal.

When a young hopeful climbs up from LEVEL D with nowhere to sleep and no seat to their name, it is often a Lares door that opens first, and when they finally move on to Level B glory, they tend to send a card home. That is the whole meaning of the household guardians: not to win the crown, but to make sure the ladder has a warm rung in the middle for everyone still climbing. Lares will never be Madonna and never wants to be; it wants only to endure, to shelter, and to finish. In a world of crowns and floors, it is the hearth that never goes cold.
quotes: Principal (warm, unhurried): 'Let Madonna keep the crown and let Bullets keep sprinting, I'll keep the lamp lit. Every driver on this grid comes home to Lares sooner or later.'
The garage (wiping down the hearth-red bodywork): 'She's not the fastest girl on the grid, but she'll bring you back every single time. Feed her, love her, she finishes. That's the Lares way.'
A rival's dig (Bullets crewman, smirking): 'Lares? Sweet old house. We'll be up in Level B chasing Senna while they're still tucking rookies into bed at the back of the midfield.'
A returning driver: 'I won my seat in Level B, but the card on my mantel still reads Lares. You don't forget the house that took you in when you had nothing.'

team.feet.name: Feet
team.feet.motto: STAND ON YOUR OWN TWO FEET, AND OUT-WORK THE WHOLE GRID.
history: Feet was never born in a boardroom, it was hammered together on a cold concrete workshop floor by men who had spent their lives wrenching for richer teams and grown sick of polishing somebody else's crown. They pooled their tools, their savings, and their stubbornness, rolled a car out of a rented lock-up with barely a sticker on the nose, and dared the Super Monaco GP circus to laugh. The paddock wits took one look at the outfit with no money and no pedigree and sneered that it "ran on its feet", so the grafters grabbed a brush, painted the name on the car themselves, and made a badge of the insult. On the ladder they landed at LEVEL C, the honest midfield, and there they have planted their boots ever since.

Where the grandees glide, Feet GRINDS. This is the working man's team of the whole circus, up before the tannoy crackles, boots on the concrete, every last part hand-fettled because there is no chequebook in the drawer to buy a shortcut. They do not bluff, they do not cheat, and they never whine; when a Feet car crosses the line ahead of a machine that cost ten times as much, the whole pit lane knows it was earned down to the final bolt. The grandstand may not chant their name, but the mechanics up and down the paddock quietly tip their caps, because everyone in this game started with dirty hands once, and Feet is the team that never let itself forget it.

The car is a plain, honest thing: steel-blue and grease-white, hand-beaten panels still wearing the fingerprints of the men who shaped them, a worn workboot stencilled on the flank where richer teams splash a champagne sponsor. It is no rocket, on raw pace the LEVEL C machine gives best to the crown's aristocrats every single Saturday, and the team will tell you so to your face. But it is TOUGH, screwed together to run the full distance and drag itself home on its rims when flashier cars are already smoking at the treeline. The garage calls her "Old Boots," and Old Boots has a stubborn habit of still being there at the flag long after the pretty machines have kissed the Armco.

Feet's whole world is the LEVEL C scrap, the ambitious midfield where reputations are forged in the muck rather than handed down at birth. It eyes the flash of the Bullets garage with a working man's narrow-eyed suspicion: let G. Ceara chase the king in that hungry insurgent machine, Feet shrugs, we'll take the honest points and the honest pay, thanks. Its lead man of the modern era was the desperate J. Rampal, #24, a driver who claws for every last tenth as though the seat is the only thing keeping his lights on, because for a Feet man, it very nearly is. And when some hotshot climbs out of a Minarae looking to make a name, it is Feet's battered blue car they have to out-drive first to prove they belong.

There is no championship banner hanging in the Feet lock-up, and the team would be the first to admit it, LEVEL C is simply not where crowns are won. But it is where they are EARNED, and Feet measures its glory in the only currency a grinder trusts: a points finish stolen from a grandee who overcooked it, a podium snatched on a chaos afternoon when the aristocrats fed the barriers, a single rung of the ladder climbed on pure merit. The dream, spoken low over a cup of garage tea, is LEVEL B, to graft their way upward one honest result at a time until the establishment has to shuffle aside and make room. Until that day comes, Feet does exactly what Feet has always done: keeps its boots on the ground, its head down, and out-works every silver spoon on the grid.
quotes: PRINCIPAL: "We've no money and no famous name, so we spend the one thing the rich can't buy back, honest graft. You want a fairy tale, go buy a Madonna. You want a car that's still running at the flag? Come and see us on the concrete."

CHIEF MECHANIC: "Every panel on Old Boots was beaten by hand on this very floor, by these very hands. I don't care what the big board says our budget is, nobody out-works this garage, and nobody on that ladder ever has."

J. RAMPAL, then the #24: "This seat is all I've got, so I fight for every point like it's my last one. Feet gave me a chair when the grandees wouldn't even look, I'll drag this blue car home on its rims before I hand it back."

A GRANDEE'S DIG: "The little team that 'runs on its feet', how perfectly quaint. No crown, no coin, just grease under the fingernails and a boot painted on the door. Charming, truly. Now kindly move the scruffy blue thing out of my mirrors."

team.serga.name: Serga
team.serga.motto: NO FACTORY BEHIND US, JUST THE WHOLE GRID AHEAD!
history: Serga is not a company, it is a garage with a grudge and a gate that opens onto pit lane. There is no boardroom, no famous crest, no cheque from on high: there is Eric Sambena of Andorra, a mountain kid who welded his own dream into a chassis and entered it under his own name. The #25 was built by hands you could count on two, in a workshop that smells of oil and stubbornness, and it arrived at the World Championship uninvited. When the establishment asks who backs Serga, the answer is nobody, and that is exactly the point.

Everything about Serga is scavenged, sharpened, and earned. The crew rebuilds tonight what the aristocrats simply replace, patches what the works teams bin, and turns a shoestring into a stopwatch that does not lie. They race on grit and instinct, reading the track by feel because they cannot afford to read it any other way. The paddock's polished set look down their noses at the little grey car, right up until it is filling their mirrors at the hairpin.

This is LEVEL C, the ambitious middle of the ladder where a name is forged or forgotten, and Serga has clawed every rung of the way up from the scrapheap of the floor. Every point on the D.P. board is banked, never bought; every place gained is proof that a self-made outfit belongs among the salaried and the sponsored. Serga does not dream of the crown the way the front-runners do, that is not yet its fight. Its fight is simpler and fiercer: to make the whole grid admit that a garage can race, and to keep climbing until someone up top loses a seat to a man who built his own.

The machine is a working man's weapon. The g3m4 chassis wears a hand-finished coat of workshop grey, a single mountain-blue stripe brushed on by Sambena himself, and a number 25 that is a little crooked because a tired man painted it at two in the morning. It rattles where the factory cars purr, it is stitched together where they are cast whole, but it is honest, it is quick, and above all it is theirs. When it crosses the line ahead of a car worth ten of it, the grandstand roars for the underdog every single time.

Serga's rivalries are the sweetest kind: the resentment of everyone it was never supposed to beat. The LEVEL A crowd bristle at losing a corner to a privateer, and the works midfield hate that the garage embarrasses their budget. With fellow outsider Bullets there is a wary respect, two LEVEL C outfits clawing upward, though where Ceara's mob hunts the crown outright, Serga hunts respect and a seat it can steal. For a driver climbing the ladder by their own hand, Serga is the mirror and the door: prove you are self-made enough, and the #25 is yours to take.
quotes: Sambena, owner and driver, wiping his hands on a rag: 'They ask who signs my cheques. I sign my cheques. I build the car, I paint the number, I drive it flat out, and when I pass a factory machine, that is the only sponsorship I need.'

From the two-man Serga garage, past midnight: 'We don't have spares, we have last night's part and a prayer. But she'll fire in the morning, and she'll be quicker than something that cost fifty times as much. Grey car, big heart!'

A LEVEL A principal, sniffing at the timing screen: 'Serga? A shed with a steering wheel. Charming little story, until that grubby number 25 turns up in my driver's mirrors, and then nobody in my garage is laughing.'
A rival engineer, grudging: 'You can out-spend a lot of things in this championship. You cannot out-spend a man who has nothing to lose and built the whole thing himself. Watch the #25, it bites.'

team.rigel.name: Rigel
team.rigel.motto: THE STAR STILL BURNS, YOU'RE JUST SEEING IT LATE.
history: Rigel takes its name from the great blue giant that hangs in the winter sky, the star so distant that the light reaching the grandstands tonight left it lifetimes ago. That is the whole tragedy of this team in a single word. There was an age, older drivers swear, when Rigel's midnight machines flickered near the front and the pit wall smelled of champagne instead of solvent. No one on the current roster was there to see it, and yet everyone races as though they might catch that light again if they only reach far enough.

The workshop is half garage, half museum, and the two halves argue. Trophies from a brighter century line a shelf that nobody dusts, angled just so they catch the work-lamps and throw a little false glory over the floor. The mechanics are lifers, grey at the temples, deft at the wrists, fluent in a car nobody else remembers how to fix. Rigel does not rebuild; Rigel restores, coaxing one more clean lap out of parts held together by memory and stubbornness. It is the most dignified poverty on the grid.

Glory, here, is a thing measured in near-misses and framed on the wall. The team's whole faith rests on one storied night at the old street circuit, when a Rigel car led into the final third before the engine let go in a plume of blue smoke that matched the paint, the podium they never quite reached, retold until it shines brighter than any that actually happened. That single almost is the fuel of the place. Down at LEVEL D the reality is harder: qualifying is a fight for the last rows, and a points finish is a festival. But Rigel keeps reaching, because a faded star is still a star.

The machine is the formula_classic G3M2, and Rigel dresses it like a relic worth loving, deep midnight blue, near black in the shadows, with a single cold white star blazing on the nose and a thin silver pinstripe tracing the flanks like a constellation drawn by hand. It is, everyone admits, one of the most beautiful cars in the paddock and one of the least reliable; the paint has gone matte with age and the engine keeps its own superstitions about when it feels like finishing. Beautiful and brittle, gorgeous and gone-cold, the livery tells you exactly what you're getting before the lights even drop.

Rigel sits at the floor of the ladder, one cruel rung above Zeroforce and the nothing beneath it, and every season is a quiet war not to slip that final step into oblivion. Far, far above, Madonna's yellow-and-red crown burns for A. Senna like the living star Rigel used to be, while brash upstarts like Bullets claw upward with a hunger this team traded away decades ago. Rookies climbing out of Minarae pass through these back rows on their way up, and Rigel takes a strange pride in being the honorable obstacle they beat, the old blue ghost that teaches the young what respect costs. They are not chasing the title. They are chasing the light. And the light, they insist, is still up there.
quotes: Principal, coat buttoned against the cold: 'They tell me the star I'm named for burned out ages ago. Fine. Then explain why I can still see it every night I look up, and why we're still on this grid.'
The garage, wiping blue paint from a knuckle: 'She'll finish if she loves you. Some nights she loves you. We build the whole weekend around finding out which night it is.'
A Bullets mechanic, smirking down the pit lane: 'Rigel? Gorgeous car. Prettiest thing at the back of the photo. We'll wave as we go by, twice, if the seat's up for grabs.'
Principal, at the trophy shelf: 'We don't rebuild here, son. We restore. There's a difference, and one day the whole grid's going to remember which of us knew it.'

team.cool.name: Cool
team.cool.motto: THE PADDOCK SWEATS. WE STAY COOL.
history: Cool was founded on a shrug and a shoestring, in a sun-warmed lockup at the cheap end of the paddock. The story goes that a burned-out old journeyman looked hard at the championship, decided all that winning was far too much like hard work, and started a team dedicated instead to simply turning up, going racing, and enjoying the ride. There was never a war chest, never a grand plan, never a whisper of the crown, only a stack of deckchairs, a cooler of cold drinks, and one battered car that ran. Down here on LEVEL D, where careers run out of road, Cool decided the trick was to never once let the panic in.

They are, without argument, the calmest garage in the whole circus. While the grandees fret over their telemetry and the strivers gnaw their fingernails to the knuckle, the Cool crew set out the deckchairs, slip on their sunglasses, and watch the light go gold over the pit lane. A single point sends them into raptures; a top-ten is a festival; a finish at all is a fine afternoon's work. The big board can say whatever it likes, Cool has never once read it in a hurry.

Make no mistake, the back of the grid is a hard place, and Cool feels every bit of it. The money is thin, the spares are thinner, and the car has an unhappy habit of coughing to a halt at the worst possible moment. But where flashier outfits have burned bright and folded, Cool is still here, season after season, patched and painted and grinning, a survivor on the floor that simply refuses to fret itself out of existence. That is their glory: not the podium, but the endurance. Down here, staying is winning.

The machine is a hand-me-down single-seater, bought used, run tired, and loved anyway. They dress it in glacier-blue and cream with a single band of sunset-orange down the flank, a pair of painted sunglasses on the nose, and a look so laid-back it reads less like a race car than a postcard from the coast. It is nobody's idea of a weapon, but it is the friendliest thing on the grid, and the crowd adores it. When the Cool car putters by, dead last and perfectly content, the whole grandstand smiles.

Their standard-bearer of the modern era was A. Delvaux, the dreaming Belgian of the #28, a romantic who calls every hairpin a love letter and swears that one day the Monaco sunset will light his podium. He has never won; he was LEVEL D, and the crown was a universe away. But when the rain sheeted across his home forest and the plucky Belgian hung on for a points finish in front of his own people, the whole place roared as if a title had been settled. Cool sits a comfortable notch above the true abyss, Zeroforce lies below, where there is nothing left at all, and there they are happy to stay: unbothered, cheap, cheerful, and utterly, gloriously cool. Let the hungry claw at the ladder, Cool will be right here, feet up, watching the light turn gold.
quotes: Points? Podium? We brought the car home in one piece, the sun's out, and nobody's crying in the garage. That, my friend, is a championship day.
She's held together with tape, good humour, and whatever was left in the parts bin, and she still starts. Well. Most mornings.
The others chase the crown. I chase the sunset, and one fine evening at Monaco, it will be waiting for me on the top step.
Cool aren't racing, they're on vacation. Somebody fetch a licence for that deckchair and clear the road for people who actually want it.

team.comet.name: Comet
team.comet.motto: ONE BLAZE ACROSS THE SKY, AND WE STILL BURN FOR IT.
history: Comet was born from a single, gorgeous accident. A back-road tuning shop with a black machine nobody rated, they arrived at the floor of LEVEL D expecting to make up the numbers, and instead caught lightning for one unrepeatable season. Their comet-tailed cars streaked past aristocrats who never bothered to learn the name on the entry list. For one summer the whole grid looked up. It is the only summer anybody remembers, and Comet has been chasing its own shadow ever since.

The character of the team is the character of a memory that refuses to cool. Comet does not brag the way the loud D-runners do, it lets the old highlight reel do the talking, then wheels the same tired chassis onto the grid and prays the magic comes back. Every mechanic in that garage was there for The Season, or swears they were. They are romantics stranded at the bottom of the ladder, a flash in the pan that never once accepted the pan went cold. Hope, here, is a maintenance schedule.

The machine is the Nero Cometa, matte black flanks with a streaking silver tail that flares to burnt orange over the sidepods, a livery built to look fast even on the laps it isn't. It runs light and eager for two flying laps, then the reliability betrays it: a fragile constitution, a bare spares shelf, and a habit of expiring in a plume of smoke somewhere past half-distance. On its day it can still steal a shock qualifying slot and make the timing screens gasp. On every other day it is a relic wearing the paint of a legend.

Comet's place on the grid is the long tail of its own brilliance, perched just above Zeroforce's bottomless floor, close enough to smell the exit door swinging open. Their feuds are the small, bitter basement kind: elbows-out scraps with Moon and Orchis over the final scoring seats, and a standing quarrel with Cool over which of the two peaked hardest and fell the furthest. Quiet Ethan Tornio at car #29 let his lap times argue for him in the team's modern era, dragging one more result out of the smoke each weekend. It is survival dressed as glory.

Above them the ladder is a wall they climbed exactly once. Madonna's crown and A. Senna are a rumor from a country Comet visited for a single week and got politely deported from; the risen Bullets and G. Ceara are the kind of insurgent hunger Comet used to have before the tank ran dry. The team knows the arithmetic, four bad weekends down here and the career-over letter arrives, no team below to catch you. So they keep the black car pointed at the horizon, chasing the streak of light they left up there once, betting the whole garage that a comet comes back around.
quotes: PRINCIPAL: 'They call us one-season wonders. Fine. We had our season, how many of these midfield ghosts can say that? The comet comes back around. You just have to survive long enough to be standing here when it does.'

GARAGE (chief mechanic, wiping oil off the Nero Cometa): 'She'll give you two laps that'll make you weep, then she'll give you a smoke signal. I rebuild the same engine every fortnight and I'd do it forever, because I was there the year she flew. You don't forget flying.'
DRIVER, E. TORNIO, then the #29: '...Not much to say. Watch the hairpin. The car's old, the tail's still fast. That's the whole story.'
