RIVAL DIG, a LEVEL A pit boss: 'Comet? Oh, the shooting star. Lovely for a night. But you name a thing after a comet, you're admitting out loud that it already burned up. We race for crowns down the years, they race for one old photograph.'

team.orchis.name: Orchis
team.orchis.motto: ONE MORE BLOOM BEFORE THE FROST!
history: They say Orchis was never really founded, it was PLANTED. Legend in the paddock annuals tells of a glasshouse magnate who loved rare flowers more than fast cars, and who potted a race team the way he potted his prize orchids: under glass, watered by hand, sheltered from every draft. The pale machine rolled out of a converted greenhouse on the cold edge of the grid, and the whole paddock laughed at the perfume of it. But it started. It qualified. And for one shimmering, impossible season, the delicate thing actually FLOWERED, a memory the team has been chasing down the ladder ever since.

Understand the character of Orchis and you understand a hothouse bloom: exquisite when everything is perfect, and utterly doomed when it is not. Give the car cool air, a smooth track, and a gentle hand, and it corners like petals opening to the sun. Ask it to fight in the heat, in the shove, in the rain of a real dogfight, and it WILTS, the engine sighs, the tyres go soft as bruised fruit, and the beautiful thing folds. This is the tragedy the crew lives with every single weekend: they have built the loveliest car nobody can quite race.

So Orchis lives on the floor now, a LEVEL D team with its roots clinging to the last crumbling ledge of the ladder. Every Sunday is a survival act, one blown radiator, one missed cut, and the drop yawns open beneath them, where Zeroforce waits at the very bottom with nothing below it. They are not the worst; they will tell you that, quickly, almost pleading. But they know the frost is coming for the tender ones first, and they can feel it on the back of the neck, and they keep telling themselves that survivors get to bloom again next spring.

The MACHINE is the softest sight on the grid, a pale orchid-lilac hull washed with dusk-pink, a single petal curling back along each sidepod, the whole thing looking less like a race car and more like a corsage that learned to drive. It is gorgeous and it is FRAGILE: the cooling runs hot, the suspension is delicate as a stem, and the mechanics handle it like something that might bruise. Rivals in their armor-plated brutes roar past and the little flower shivers in the wash. Beautiful in the brochure; terrifying in the braking zone.

Where does a wilting flower fit on a ladder built for wolves? Orchis looks UP and sees an entire world it can barely dream of, the aristocrats of LEVEL A, and far above even them the untouchable yellow-and-red crown of Madonna and A. Senna, a summit on another planet. It looks ACROSS and sees the hungry young things climbing out of Minarae and clawing up through the midfield, any one of whom could shove Orchis off its ledge for sport. And it looks DOWN, always down, at the drop into Zeroforce. The team's entire ambition has shrunk to a single, defiant, heartbreaking sentence: not to win, just to survive long enough to bloom one more time.
quotes: The principal, softly, watering can still in hand: 'People call us delicate like it is an insult. My friends, ANYTHING worth loving is delicate. We only need one perfect afternoon, cool air, clean track, a gentle line, and this little flower will open in front of the whole world. Give me that, and give me one more season.'

A mechanic, wiping steam off his goggles: 'She overheated on the warm-up lap. The WARM-UP lap. I don't tune this car, mate, I GARDEN it, trim the wilted bits, prop up the stem, whisper to it, and pray the frost holds off till the flag.'

A rival from up the ladder, sneering out the visor: 'Orchis? Prettiest thing on the grid, sure, right up until the first corner, when it folds like a bouquet somebody sat on. Careful you don't crush the petals on your way to the back, darling.'
The team's number one, quiet after another Sunday clinging on: 'We finished. We are still here. Down here that is not a result you laugh at, that is the whole championship.'

team.moon.name: Moon
team.moon.motto: SILVER, COLD, AND CIRCLING, BUT EVERY MOON IS ON THE RISE.
history: Moon is born on the far edge of the paddock, the last outfit to bolt its gates shut before the season deadline, funded on fumes and stubborn dreaming. There is no dynasty here, no trophy cabinet, no golden year the old hands whisper about, only a pale silver machine and a promise that one day, somehow, this team will rise. From the very first grid it lines up near the floor of LEVEL D, distant and quiet, a cold little dot the front-runners barely notice in their mirrors. But Moon does not fold. Season after season it keeps its lights on and keeps circling, and that alone, down here at the bottom, is a kind of victory.

The character of Moon is cold, aloof, and strangely serene, a garage that speaks in low voices and points its telescope at horizons the paddock finds absurd. It never shines with its own fire; it runs, its people say, on borrowed light, catching a little reflected glory whenever a leader laps them and the cameras swing by. It is the far side of the grid, the part nobody watches, orbiting patiently through race after race while the establishment burns bright up front. There is no bitterness in it, only distance, the calm of a team that measures its life in slow, faithful laps rather than podiums.

Life on the LEVEL D floor is a hard, thankless circle, and Moon knows every inch of it. The number 30 is lapped by the crown before the halfway flag, the gearbox groans, the budget runs colder than the livery, and the points column stays as empty as deep space. Only Zeroforce sits below them in the whole ladder, and some cruel weekends the gap to daylight feels like a gap between planets. Yet in the team's modern era Kevin Yepes climbed out grinning every single time, waving cheerfully at the machines that lapped him, insisting the back row had the finest view on the grid. That impossible good cheer, more than any result, is the fuel that keeps Moon's far-off dream alive.

The machine is the trusty, weary g3m4 dressed in cold lunar silver-grey, matte and dust-flecked, pocked like a cratered surface, trimmed in deep night-blue with the number 30 glowing pale on the nose. It is no rocket; it is a patient, chrome-cold thing built to finish rather than to fly, to keep circling long after flashier cars have broken. The garage calls their pit-wall gambles 'moonshots' and means it fondly, every long-odds strategy is a hand reaching up toward a light impossibly far away. When the silver flashes under the Monaco lamps, the crowd calls it the loneliest, prettiest car on the grid, and Moon takes that as the compliment it is.

In the ladder's cold arithmetic, Moon orbits low, tethered near Zeroforce at the very bottom, gazing up at a crown that hangs light-years out of reach. Far, far above them Madonna's yellow-and-red machine wears the crown that never slips, and even the insurgent surge of Bullets clawing up from LEVEL C looks, from down here, like a comet blazing to a heaven Moon can only dream of. There is no true rivalry for a team this distant, only the pull of that far bright grid and the quiet, unkillable wish to rise toward it, one phase at a time. They do not hate the front-runners; they study them, plot their slow ascent, and wait for the night their silver finally climbs. Until then Moon keeps its cold vigil at the back, circling, dreaming, always on the rise.
quotes: Principal: 'We are the far side of this grid, cold, quiet, and forever circling, and I would not trade it. Let the crown blaze away up front; a moon does not chase the sun. It waits through the dark, holds its orbit, and rises in its own good time. Ours is coming.'

Garage chief: 'She runs on borrowed light and spare parts, this silver girl of ours. Point her at the hairpin, wave to the leaders as they lap us, and dream a size bigger every single night. That's the whole job, friend, keep her circling till the phase turns.'

Kevin Yepes, then the #30: 'Last place has the best view of the entire grid, and don't let anyone tell you different! I'm cold, I'm slow, I'm going in circles at the back, and I am having the absolute time of my life up here in orbit!'

Rival dig (a crown-team driver): 'Moon? That's the little silver speck I lap twice before lunch. Round and round it goes, dreaming of the rise, pal, from down there even LEVEL C is another planet. Enjoy the view of my gearbox.'

team.zeroforce.name: Zeroforce
team.zeroforce.motto: ZEROFORCE, THE END OF THE ROAD HAS A PIT BOX, AND IT'S OURS.
history: ZEROFORCE was not so much founded as it was ASSEMBLED, bolted together in the last, coldest garage at the dead end of pit lane, from the parts and people nobody else wanted. Legend in the paddock says the team took its name the night its first machine failed to move off the line under its own power: zero force, zero go, zero excuses. Where the crowned houses of LEVEL A speak of dynasties and destiny, Zeroforce speaks of ONE more lap, ONE more start, ONE more month with the lights still on. It is the floor of the grid, and everyone in this sport knows that when the floor is where you land, there is nothing below.

The character of this team is written in GREY. There is no swagger here, no golden benchmark, no talk of the crown, only the grim, spent dignity of a crew that has seen a hundred careers arrive full of fire and leave in silence. Drivers do not come to Zeroforce to be discovered; they come because every other door in the paddock has already shut. The garage runs on cold coffee, gallows humor, and the stubborn refusal to be classified DEAD LAST just one more weekend. It is the end of the line, and it wears that title like a scar it has stopped hiding.

Glory is a word they do not use in this box, because at LEVEL D the scoreboard measures something crueler than winning: SURVIVAL. A Zeroforce weekend is a triumph if the machine qualifies, a miracle if it finishes, and a full-blown celebration if it is not the last car running when the flag falls. They chase the strugglers ahead of them, the flickering lights of Rigel, Cool, Comet, Orchis and Moon, not for podiums but for the sweet, thin oxygen of being one place OFF the bottom. Every point this team has ever scored was clawed from a machine that had no business still turning, and they remember each one by name.

The machine itself is a battered, blacked-out thing, matte charcoal gone to rust at the seams, a livery the color of a garage after the lights go out, bearing a stark white ZERO that the crew swears is a number and rivals swear is a verdict. It sounds wrong on the warm-up lap, and it sounds worse under load, but it goes, and at LEVEL D, GOING is the whole religion. There is no sponsor gloss, no polished nose cone, just tape, weld, and a paint job that dares the field to look away first. It is not beautiful. It is STILL HERE, which down here means the same thing.

In the great ladder of this sport, Zeroforce is the mirror the whole grid tries not to look into. High above, MADONNA and the untouchable A. Senna own a crown that never slips; further down, hungry BULLETS and G. Ceara claw UPWARD toward that crown from LEVEL C, all fire and future. Zeroforce is the opposite arrow, the direction no driver plans to travel, the last exit before the road simply ends. Its true rivalry is not with any one team but with OBLIVION itself, and every race it starts is another day it stares that rival down and, somehow, refuses to blink.
quotes: Principal: 'People ask me what our goal is this season. Our goal is that there IS a next season. You want dynasties, go knock on Madonna's golden door, down here we count the ones who are still breathing at the flag, and every so often, that's us.'

Garage: 'She sounds like a dying furnace and drives like a shopping trolley full of bricks, but she MOVES, and on this rung of the ladder, moving is the only word that matters. Tape it, weld it, send it. There's no truck coming to save us.'

Garage: 'Nobody dreams of a Zeroforce seat, kid. You end up in one. But I'll tell you this for nothing, the crews up top have never once had to fight for a single lap the way we fight for ALL of them.'

Rival's dig (from a LEVEL A house): 'Zeroforce? Darling, that's not a team, that's the DRAIN the grid slides into. When your career runs out of road, that grey little box is the sound it makes hitting the bottom.'
